analogy: Think of "Change and Development in Industrial Society" through the chapter's own opening images: Change and Development in Industrial Society 5 Social Change and Development in India 60 Bollywood in Mumbai, Maharashtra may be a place of dreams for you and me, but for many, it is their place of work. Like any industry, the workers there are part... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 5.1 Images of IndustrIal socIety, 5.2 IndustrIalIsatIon In IndIa, 5.3 How People FInd Jobs, 5.4 How Is work carrIed out?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Change and Development in Industrial Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 5

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 60 Bollywood in Mumbai, Maharashtra may be a place of dreams for you and me, but for many, it is their place of work.

The text explains that like any industry, the workers there are part of unions.

The text explains that for instance, the dancers, stunt artists and the extras are all part of a junior artists association, whose demands include 8 hours shifts, proper wages and safe working conditions.

The text explains that the products of this industry are advertised and marketed through film distributors and cinema hall owners or through shops in the form of music cassettes and videos.

The text explains that and the people who work in this industry, as in any other, live in the same city, but depending on who they are and how much they earn, they do very different things in that city.

The text explains that film stars and textile mill owners live in places like Juhu, while extras and textile workers may live in places like Girangaon.

The text explains that some go to five star hotels and eat Japanese sushi and some eat vada pav from the local handcart.

The text explains that the residents of Mumbai are divided by where they live, what they eat and how much their clothes cost.

The text explains that but they are also united by certain common things that a city provides – they watch the same films and cricket matches, they suffer from the same air pollution and they all have aspirations for their children to do well.

The text explains that how and where people work and what kind of jobs they have is an important part of who they are.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will see how changes in technology or the kind of work that is available has changed social relations in India.

The text explains that on the other hand, social institutions like caste, kinship networks, gender and region also influence the way that work is organised or the way in which products are marketed.

The text explains that this is a major area of research for sociologists.

The text explains that for instance, why do we find more women in certain jobs like nursing or teaching than in other sectors like engineering?

The text explains that is this just a coincidence or is it because society thinks that women are suited for caring and nurturing work as against jobs which are seen as ‘tough’ and masculine?

The text explains that sociologists are interested in the questions of who produces what, who works where, who sells to whom and how.

The text explains that in turn, the choices that people make influences how society works.

The text explains that 5.1 Images of IndustrIal socIety Many of the great works of sociology were written at a time when industrialisation was new and machinery was assuming great importance.

The text explains that thinkers like Karl Marx, Max Weber and Emile Durkheim associated a number of social features with industry, such as urbanisation, the loss of face-to-face relationships that were found in rural areas where people worked on their own farms or

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 61 for a landlord they knew, and their substitution by anonymous professional relationships in modern factories and workplaces.

The text explains that according to the convergence thesis put forward by modernisation theorist Clark Kerr, an industrialised India of the 21st century shares more features with China or the United States in the 21st century than it shares with 19th century India.

The text explains that these have to be registered with the government to ensure that their employees get proper salaries or wages, pension and other benefits.

The text explains that of those who do, two-thirds work for the government.

The text explains that this is why people strive hard to get into government jobs.

The text explains that government employment in India has played a major role in overcoming boundaries of caste, religion and region.

The text explains that one sociologist has argued that the reason why there have never been communal riots in a place like Bhilai is because the public sector Bhilai Steel Plant employs people from all over India who work together.

The text explains that third, since very few people are members of unions, a feature of the organised sector, the unorganised or informal sector workers do not have the experience of collectively fighting for proper wages and safe working conditions.

The text explains that the government has laws to monitor conditions in the unorganised sector, but in practice they are left to the whims and fancies of the employer or contractor.

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 63 GlobaliSaTion, liberaliSaTion and ChanGeS in indian InduSTry Since the 1990s, the government has followed a policy of liberalisation.

The text explains that private companies, especially foreign firms, are encouraged to invest in sectors earlier reserved for the government, including telecom, civil aviation, power, etc.

The text explains that the government is trying to sell its share in several public sector companies, a process which is known as disinvestment.

The text explains that many government workers are scared that after disinvestment, they will lose their jobs.

The text explains that in ‘Modern Foods’, which was set up by the government to make healthy bread available at cheap prices, and which was the first company to be privatised, 60% of the workers were forced to retire in the first five years.

The text explains that almost all companies, even government ones, now practice some form of outsourcing and contracting.

The text explains that so far, employment by the government was a major avenue for increasing the well-being of the population, but now even that is coming down.

The text explains that you will be reading more about this in the next chapter on globalisation.

The text explains that at the same time as secure employment in large industry is declining, the government is embarking on a policy of land acquisition for industry.

The text explains that you will recall the discussion on competing interests from Chapter 3.

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 65 the mistri also put community-related pressures on the worker.

The text explains that employment opportunities have two important components: (i) job in an organisation as a casual wage labourer or regular salaried (ii) Self-employment Recently the Government of India floated many schemes, such as ‘MUDRA’, ‘Aatmanirbhar Bharat’ and ‘Make in India’ in order to generate employment and self-employment to support all sections including the marginalised section of the society, like SC, ST and other backward classes.

The text explains that however, the contractor system is most visible in the hiring of casual labour for work at construction sites, brickyards, and so on.

The text explains that in that sense, they are more free in an industrial society.

The text explains that i want the concentration of wealth, not in the hands of the few, but in the hands of all.” 1934: “When as a nation we adopt the spinning- wheel, we not only solve the question of unemployment but we declare that we have no intention of exploiting any nation, and we also end the exploitation of the poor by the rich.” Give an example of how machinery creates a problem for workers.

The text explains that the firm has outsourced all services like cleaning, and security, as well as the manufacture of parts.

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 67 Now let us look at the services sector.

The text explains that but, as we see from the box, their work is also subject to Taylorist labour processes.

The text explains that one important debate in sociology is whether industrialisation and the shift to services and knowledge-based work, like IT, leads to greater skills in society.

The text explains that we often hear the phrase ‘knowledge economy’ to describe the growth of IT sector in India.

The text explains that 1999. ‘The Study of Industrial Labour in post-colonial India – The Formal Sector: An Introductory review’.

The text explains that 1999. ‘The Study of Industrial Labour in post-colonial India – The Informal Sector: A concluding review’.

The text explains that down and Out: Labouring Under Global Capitalism.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 5: Change and Development in Industrial Society

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Change and Development in Industrial Society, which statement is supported by the source text about Change?
options: Industrialisation leads to greater equality, at least in some spheres.
For example, caste distinctions do not matter any more on trains, buses or in cyber cafes.
On the other hand, older forms of discrimination may persist even in new factory or workplace settings.
And even as social inequalities are reducing, economic or income inequality is growing in the world.
